items.question: Who developed t-SNE and for what purpose?
items.answer: Laurens van der Maaten and Geoffrey Hinton introduced t-SNE (2008), extending Stochastic Neighbor Embedding by using a Student-t kernel in the low-dimensional space to improve visualization of high-dimensional data by preserving local neighborhoods.

items.question: What objective does t-SNE minimize?
items.answer: t-SNE minimizes KL divergence KL(P‖Q) between symmetrized high-dimensional Gaussian affinities p_ij and low-dimensional Student-t affinities q_ij, adjusting embedding coordinates y_i via gradient descent so similar input points remain close in the map.

items.question: When should you use t-SNE instead of PCA?
items.answer: Use t-SNE for non-linear 2D/3D visualization when local neighborhood structure matters and manifolds may be curved; use PCA for linear compression, interpretable loadings, and fast preprocessing—often PCA-to-50D before t-SNE on large p.

items.question: Where does perplexity appear in the t-SNE algorithm?
items.answer: Perplexity sets the target entropy of each point's conditional Gaussian over neighbors; for every x_i the algorithm searches bandwidth σ_i so p(·|i) has the requested effective neighborhood size before symmetrizing to p_ij.

items.question: Why does t-SNE use a Student-t distribution in the embedding space?
items.answer: Heavy tails of the Student-t (1 dof) relieve the crowding problem: many moderate-distance high-D neighbors can be represented at larger low-D separations without all collapsing near the center, which Gaussian low-D kernels caused in original SNE.

items.question: How do you run and tune t-SNE in a typical sklearn workflow?
items.answer: Scale features if needed, optionally PCA to ~50 dimensions, then TSNE(n_components=2, perplexity≈5–50, init='pca', random_state fixed) and fit_transform on the visualization set; sweep perplexity, report hyperparameters, and plot with optional label coloring for EDA only.

items.question: t-SNE vs PCA for dimensionality reduction: what differs?
items.answer: PCA is linear, deterministic, preserves global variance, and produces reusable components for modeling; t-SNE is non-linear, stochastic, optimizes local affinities for 2D/3D plots, and distorts global distances—PCA compresses features, t-SNE visualizes them.

items.question: t-SNE vs UMAP for embedding visualization: what trade-off?
items.answer: Both target non-linear structure; t-SNE emphasizes local neighborhoods strongly and can distort global layout, while UMAP often retains more global topology and scales better—compare both with fixed preprocessing when publishing exploratory maps.

items.question: Cause: perplexity is set very low on a noisy dataset. Effect on the t-SNE map?
items.answer: Each point focuses on very few neighbors, producing fragmented micro-clusters and overemphasis of local noise; the map may show many tiny islands rather than stable group-level structure until perplexity is raised.

items.question: Cause: features are not scaled and one column has much larger variance. Effect on affinities?
items.answer: Euclidean distances are dominated by that feature, so Gaussian bandwidths and p_ij reflect scale artifacts rather than balanced multi-feature similarity—neighborhoods skew unless StandardScaler or domain-appropriate scaling is applied first.

items.question: What breaks t-SNE as a reliable measure of inter-cluster separation?
items.answer: Using large gaps between distant blobs as quantitative evidence of class separability breaks down because t-SNE does not preserve global distances—only local affinities are optimized, so cluster spacing and size can change across runs and perplexity settings without reflecting true feature-space distance.
